turns.text: Hello Alethia, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're adjusting to life after having your baby, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s a quiet morning here, the baby is finally settled, so I’m happy to talk. Honestly, it’s a relief to process it all out loud. You know, having been through this twice before, I thought I had the "script" down. I’ve spent years working in holistic care—I know the anatomy, the physiology, the advocacy side of things. But being on the other side of the bed this time? It was entirely different.

There was this strange, nagging pressure to prove that my body could do exactly what I tell other women it can do. I found myself checking my own vitals, almost clinical-like, because I was terrified that if I voiced any fear, my midwives might mistake my advocacy for "difficult" behavior. That’s a heavy weight to carry when you’re already in the thick of labor. 

I remember transition specifically—I was scared, and I saw that nurse glance at my birth plan. In that second, I wasn't the professional anymore; I was just a woman terrified they’d judge me for wanting to meet the pain on my own terms. It’s hard to articulate, but the biggest hurdle wasn't the labor itself—it was the fear of not being heard in my most vulnerable state. I think that’s what really matters, isn't it? Being seen, not just treated.
When you think about your goals for recovery and support in the days and weeks after birth, what did you most want or need — and how well did the postnatal care you received address those needs?

Recovery this time around felt so different from my first two. Because I’m older—well, thirty feels older when you're chasing two other kids—I just wanted space. I needed to know that my body wasn't just being viewed as a vessel that had successfully "finished the job," but that I was still a person who needed time to heal, both physically and mentally.

Honestly, in the immediate aftermath, I was desperate for someone to acknowledge the emotional tax of that birth. Most of the focus was on the baby’s weight gain or my physical healing, which I know is vital, but I felt like I was holding my breath for someone to ask how I felt about the *experience* of the birth itself. I wanted to talk about that moment in transition, about the fear that I might be seen as "difficult" for sticking to my plan. 

The care I received was clinically perfect, don't get me wrong. My midwives are wonderful women. But there’s a gap there—a disconnect between the clinical check-boxes and the emotional reality of becoming a mother for the third time. I needed more than just a physical assessment; I needed validation that the way I chose to navigate that pain was respected. It took me days to finally exhale, just watching the sunrise here, and realize I didn't have to perform for anyone anymore.
When you think about feeding your baby — whether through breastfeeding, formula, or a combination — what motivated your choices, and how supported did you feel in pursuing the approach you wanted?

Breastfeeding has always been this incredibly primal, grounding experience for me, but it’s never been simple. With my first two, I had the benefit of naivety; this time, having studied lactation support, I think I over-intellectualized it. I knew the "correct" way it was supposed to happen, and that knowledge actually made me more anxious. I felt this pressure to be the model of a successful, natural mother, which is silly when you say it out loud, but it felt real in the moment.

I chose to breastfeed because it was the one thing I felt I had total agency over after the chaos of labor. It’s my quiet time with him, watching the light hit the Taunton River, and it helps me feel like I’m reclaiming my body. 

Support-wise, I felt lucky that my midwives didn’t push me, but they also didn’t probe much. They assumed because I’m "in the field" that I didn't need guidance. Sometimes that’s isolating—it’s like being a doctor who is also a patient; they don’t want to overstep, so they offer less support than maybe I needed. I didn’t want a lecture on latch techniques, but I did want someone to sit with me and just acknowledge how exhausting the physical demand is. I think we need to move past "fed is best" or "breast is best" and just ask, "How are *you* doing while you feed him?"

When you think about your emotional and mental health in the weeks and months after birth — the adjustment, the challenges, and the moments of wellbeing — what stands out most, and how well-supported did you feel?

The mental load is the part people don't prepare you for, even when it’s your third time. The physical recovery is obvious—the soreness, the exhaustion—but the mental adjustment is like navigating a thick fog. In those first few weeks, I felt this intense, quiet pressure to keep it all together for the older kids while being the "expert" mother I’m supposed to be. There’s a specific kind of loneliness in that, especially when you feel like you’re supposed to have all the answers.

I remember one afternoon, sitting here feeling completely overwhelmed by the sensory overload of it all—the baby crying, the older kids needing things—and I just wanted to cry, not out of sadness, but out of sheer intensity. I felt like if I admitted to needing help, it would somehow invalidate my professional knowledge of how to handle birth and postpartum. 

I think the support system for mothers is still so focused on the physical. If your vitals are good and the baby is gaining, you’re checked off as "fine." But I needed someone to talk to about the transition of identity—who am I now with three children? That mental space remained largely unsupported. I’ve had to rely heavily on my husband to just sit with me and witness the transition without trying to "fix" anything. Just knowing I wasn't alone in the room was the only thing that kept me grounded.
Can you describe a period when you felt particularly low, overwhelmed, or unlike yourself — what was happening and who or what helped?

It was about ten days postpartum, right when the "new baby" adrenaline had finally worn off and reality set in. My husband had just gone back to work, and the older two were demanding, energetic, and needing a version of me that I simply couldn't conjure. I remember sitting on the floor in the living room, trying to nurse the baby while simultaneously mediating a sibling dispute, and I just… I couldn't move. I felt like I was physically vibrating with exhaustion.

The "low" wasn't just physical fatigue; it was an identity crisis. I felt like a failure because I had read all the books and I knew all the theories about postpartum bonding and "gentle parenting," yet here I was, feeling completely raw and incapable of being the patient, omniscient mother I felt I had to be. I felt like a fraud.

What helped? It wasn't a doctor or a clinical appointment. It was my husband, who came home, saw me sitting there in the mess, and didn't ask how the baby was doing or check my blood pressure. He just sat on the floor with me, took the baby, and said, "You don't have to be the expert today. You just have to be here." That validation—that it was okay to be struggling—was the first time I really let myself breathe. I needed permission to be a person, not a professional.

When you think about the shift in your identity and sense of self after becoming a parent, what tensions or changes were most significant — and how did those changes interact with your other roles, such as partner, professional, or daughter?

The tension is constant. I feel like I’m constantly pivoting between two worlds: the one where I’m an authority—where women come to me for guidance on their births—and the one where I’m just a woman in pajamas, struggling to remember the last time I showered. Before my third, I managed to balance these parts of myself fairly well. But now? The professional "Alethia" feels like a distant memory, a person I visit sometimes, while the mother is all-consuming.

It’s been hard on my marriage, too. My husband sees me as his partner, but sometimes I think he gets confused because he sees me shifting into that "expert" mode, trying to manage the household like a clinical trial. He has to remind me that he doesn't need me to troubleshoot him, he just needs me to be present. 

The biggest shift is realizing that my professional knowledge doesn't grant me immunity from the messiness of motherhood. I spent years advocating for informed choice and physiological birth, and now I’m realizing that my greatest challenge isn't navigating the system—it’s navigating the loss of my own autonomy. I’m no longer just Alethia; I’m a vessel for others, a source of nutrition, and a referee. It’s beautiful, truly, but I’m still mourning the version of myself that didn't have to be everything to everyone, all at once.
Can you describe a moment when you felt the tension between your new parental identity and another part of your identity most acutely?

It hit me about three weeks ago during a virtual meeting for a project I consult on. I was tucked away in my home office, trying to project this calm, informed, and articulate version of myself. I had my blazer on, my notes spread out, and I was deep into a discussion about maternal care protocols. And then, the baby started crying in the next room—that specific, hungry cry that pierces right through your soul.

For a split second, I was paralyzed. My professional self was trying to maintain eye contact with the camera and finish my sentence about postpartum safety, while my mother-self was physically leaning toward the door, my heart hammering in my chest. I felt like I was being pulled in half. I wasn't doing either job well in that moment. I felt entirely like a fraud—a "professional" who was just a stressed-out mom trying to hide the fact that she hadn't slept more than two hours at a time in weeks.

I had to mute my microphone, go scoop him up, and return to the screen with him tucked into a wrap, hoping nobody would notice. The tension was suffocating. I realized then that society wants us to be the seamless transitioner—the woman who births and then just "snaps back" into her career—but it's a lie. I was grieving the professional I was before the baby while simultaneously feeling guilty for not being fully present for him. I felt like I was failing at being "whole" in either world.

When you think about the support you received from your partner in the postpartum period — in caring for the baby, supporting your recovery, and sharing emotional labour — how would you describe that experience, and what would have made it better?

My husband is a good man, and he tries so hard. In those first few weeks, he really took on the physical burden—the dishes, the laundry, the older two kids' schedules. He kept the house running, which allowed me to stay in that "nursing cave" where I needed to be. But the emotional labor? That’s where things got complicated.

Because I’m the one with the clinical background, there was this unspoken expectation that I would be the "project manager" of the baby’s health. I found myself directing him on how to hold the baby, when to change a diaper, or how to soothe a cry, which just added to my cognitive load. It was exhausting. I didn't want to be a manager; I wanted to be a partner who could shut my brain off and let someone else take the reins completely.

What would have made it better? If he had felt confident enough to tell me, "I've got this, go lie down, don't worry about the instructions." I craved that proactive support—where he anticipated the need without me having to map it out for him. It’s hard to let go of the control when you’re used to being the "expert," but I needed him to step into that space so I could finally feel like a person who didn't have to be in charge of everything.
How did the division of caregiving responsibilities compare to what you and your partner had discussed or assumed before the birth?

We talked about it—we really did. We sat at the kitchen table with our coffee, spreadsheets of schedules and "what-ifs," convinced that because we’d been through this twice, we had the infrastructure to handle a third. We assumed it would be an even split, like it had eventually become with the others. But the reality? It’s been far less balanced than the plan suggested.

The tension comes from the fact that he looks to me for the "why" and the "how" behind everything. Even when he’s doing the work, he’s doing it by *my* manual. I’ll see him bottle-feeding or changing a diaper, and he’ll look up at me, waiting for confirmation that he’s doing it correctly. That wasn't what I wanted. I wanted to hand the baby over and have that part of my brain—the part that’s always monitoring, always analyzing—simply go dark. 

I think we both fell into a trap of assuming that my expertise meant I was the default lead. It’s been a shift from what we assumed would be a partnership of equals to a situation where I’m the lead and he’s the support staff, which neither of us actually wanted. It’s hard to dismantle those patterns once you’re in the thick of the exhaustion, but we’re slowly realizing that the plan we made on paper couldn't account for the sheer, overwhelming weight of the mental load.

When you think about the barriers you encountered in accessing postnatal support — whether physical, emotional, informational, or systemic — what were the most significant, and what impact did they have on your recovery and wellbeing?

The biggest barrier was, ironically, my own background. It sounds strange, but because I’m "in the field," there was an invisible wall erected between me and the help I actually needed. My providers assumed I was fine, or that I knew exactly what to do for myself, so they didn't offer the kind of gentle, probing care that a first-time mother might get. They saw the "professional" and stopped seeing the "patient."

Systemically, the postnatal window feels like a vacuum. You leave the hospital or the birth center, and it’s like you fall off a cliff. There’s no continuity—one day you have a team of experts monitoring your blood pressure and your morale, and the next day you’re sitting at home, completely isolated. That sudden drop-off is dangerous. 

The lack of emotional infrastructure was the most significant issue for me. There was no space provided to debrief the birth, no one checking in on my mental state beyond a standard depression screening form that I knew exactly how to answer to get the "all-clear." That impact was profound—it made me feel like my mental health was just another clinical marker to be ticked off, rather than a human experience to be nurtured. I think if there had been just one person—a midwife, a therapist, even a peer—who asked, "How are *you* holding up?" without a clipboard in their hand, my recovery would have felt so much less like a lonely endurance test.

When you think about how you used digital tools, apps, social media, or online communities after birth — to seek information, find support, or track your recovery — what role did those resources play, and what were their limits?

Digital tools were a double-edged sword for me. During those middle-of-the-night feeds, my phone became a portal to two very different worlds. On one hand, tracking apps were a godsend for my sleep-deprived brain—I couldn't remember what time I fed him or when I last changed a diaper to save my life, so having that data logged felt like the only thing keeping us on track.

But then there was social media. It was… heavy. I’d be scrolling and see these curated images of "empowered" motherhood—women who seemed to have figured out the secret to seamless, magical postpartum periods. It fed into that perfectionism I’ve been struggling with. It made me feel like if I wasn't thriving, I was failing at the very thing I’m supposed to be an expert in. 

The biggest limit was that none of these tools could provide true human connection. They gave me information—data points on growth, lists of symptoms—but they didn't offer a heartbeat. I could read all the articles on "postpartum wellness," but they couldn't reach through the screen and say, "It’s okay to be a mess today." They kept me in my head, analyzing and comparing, rather than allowing me to be present in my body and just *be* with my baby.
Which specific digital tools or communities did you find most valuable, and what made them useful in ways that formal care was not?

I found myself gravitating toward small, closed groups on platforms like Signal or private Facebook circles, specifically those for birth workers and midwives who are also mothers. Those spaces were different from the broader, more filtered social media feeds. They were useful in a way my clinical check-ups never were because, in those groups, the mask could slip.

I could post a photo of my messy living room or a blunt, exhausted update about my anxiety, and instead of a "clinical" response about breastfeeding positions or weight gain, I got raw, honest solidarity. People would reply with, "I’m in the same boat," or, "I remember feeling that exact same way with my third." 

That was the missing link. Formal care is very sterile—it’s focused on pathology, on making sure I’m physically "normal." But these peer communities focused on the *lived experience* of the transition. They didn't have a clipboard, and they weren't assessing me. They were witnessing me. When I felt like a fraud for struggling, reading someone else’s account of their own messy, imperfect postpartum felt like a lifeline. It didn't provide me with a medical diagnosis, but it provided me with a sense of sanity that the formal system, for all its technical excellence, just couldn't replicate.

When you reflect on your entire maternity journey — from preconception through to the postpartum period — what do you wish had been done differently by the care system, and what single change would have made the greatest difference to your experience?

If I had to pinpoint what I wish had been different, it’s that the system treated me as a *knowledgeable consumer* rather than a *person in transition*. Because of my background, the care system effectively "checked out" on the human side of things, assuming I was managing my own emotional wellbeing just because I understood the physiology. I wish they had realized that being an expert doesn’t mean you’re immune to the vulnerability of being a mother. 

If I could pick one single change that would have made the greatest difference, it would be the inclusion of a dedicated, non-clinical "debrief" session around four to six weeks postpartum. Not a medical check-up, not a weight check for the baby, but a space specifically for me to talk through the birth and the mental shift I was navigating. 

I needed someone to hold the space for me to say, "I felt terrified during transition, and I felt like a fraud for the weeks after." If a midwife or a peer had sat down with me, closed their laptop, and simply held space for that narrative—without offering advice or "fixing" me—it would have validated my experience as a human being. It would have transformed my recovery from an endurance test into a supported transition.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

You know, there’s one more thing that keeps circling back in my mind. We’ve talked so much about the pressure of being an "expert," and the isolation of being a third-time mom, but I think there's a deeper, more quiet realization I’m coming to now that the initial storm is settling.

It’s about the idea of "success" in maternity care. The system measures success by the absence of trauma, the health of the baby, the normalcy of the blood pressure, the stats. And while those things are the foundation—the absolute bedrock—they aren't the whole house. I’m realizing that the "performance" I felt trapped in wasn't just my own vanity; it was a symptom of a culture that expects women to just *snap back*. 

I want to add that even with all my knowledge, and even with a "good" birth, the transition into this third version of myself was still a loss. I’m mourning the woman who didn't have to carry the weight of three little souls, and I think we need to give ourselves permission to grieve that, too. We focus so much on the "glow" of motherhood that we don't hold enough space for the quiet, lonely, messy act of letting go of who we were to become who we are now. 

Thank you for listening. Just speaking about this, naming it, feels like a weight being lifted off my chest. It helps me see that my struggle wasn't a flaw—it was just part of the work.
